preamble: Dental or alveolar palatalization of [t] to the postalveolar affricate [tʃ] is frequently driven by a following palatal glide [j], a process known as yod-coalescence. This shift is characteristic of both historical and synchronic developments in English and many Romance varieties, where dental stops become affricates under palatal influence.

examples.note: Historical yod-coalescence where the stop and glide fused.
Synchronic coalescent palatalization in rapid speech.
Historical palatalization of the dental stop before a glide.
Allophonic palatalization before high front vowel /i/.

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Alkire, T., & Rosen, C. (2010). Romance Philology.
Wells, J. C. (1982). Accents of English.
